## [00:00 - 03:00]

Hey guys, welcome back to Module 2. This video is all about the concept of traffic temperature. And this is something that we created here at Digital Marketer to try to explain where a person that you're targeting through paid ads, where they fall on the value journey, right? And we're starting to merge the two. You can see this really simple graphic that explains traffic temperature. There are three levels. So you have cold here on the outside. Those are people who have never heard of your brand before, right? Cold traffic is what I call these people. In the middle you have warm traffic. So these are people who have engaged with your brand, but they've never really purchased. There's some sort of barrier to them taking the next step, right? So they've subscribed to your email list. They followed you on Facebook. They're warm, but they're not quite there yet. And the center, hot, these are buyers. These are people who have purchased from you in the past. And I'm going to use this language throughout this course to speak about the traffic temperature of certain audiences. Is someone cold? Are we targeting a warm audience? Are we trying to get people who have purchased in the past to buy again or to buy something else? Now, it's really important when you think about your traffic campaigns that they have a specific goal. So as we talked about in module one, the goal of your campaign shouldn't be generate leads, make money, ah, everything. You can't expect one campaign to pull that many levers. So whenever I'm thinking about a new traffic campaign, I first decide what is the goal, right? And here are a few possible goals. So maybe the goal is to introduce you to people who have never heard of your brand before, right? Awareness. This is called traffic. Maybe your goal is to convert prospects into customers, right? Acquiring new customers into your business. Maybe your goal is to upsell or to cross sell a recent buyer to get them to take another action. When I'm selecting a goal for a paid traffic campaign, it's usually one of these three, right? And these line up nicely with traffic temperature, right? Cold, warm, hot. So making sure that you understand that when you're reaching different temperatures of audiences that the results and the goals for those campaigns are going to differ because those people are at a different stage on the customer journey. So if we're to overlay traffic temperature on top of value journey, these first three steps are really your cold, your cold audiences, right? These next two are your warm audiences. And then these last three here are your hot audiences.

So just for those of you that are visual, this is how traffic temperature overlays with the value journey. So let's talk a little bit more about cold traffic, right? So cold traffic, this is the new blood, you know, coming into your business, right? So people who have never heard of you before, if you're a brand new business, you got to start here. It's also important to keep in mind that if you do have an existing business, it's okay to start with traffic campaigns that maybe reach more of your hot or warm audiences because they'll get, you know, immediate results. But if you never scale out to cold traffic, the business is not acquiring new awareness or new leads, right? ## [09:02 - 12:06]

And because he is more trusting than I am inherently, he might see that ad engage with the brand and it immediately buys something from them because that's just how he rolls, right? That's his consumer behavior. I could have the same interest but be a little less trusting, right? That's just maybe who I am as a human. So I might need a few weeks before I make a decision to buy. There are lots of factors that go into that but I know that if you create paid traffic campaigns that are there to continue to re-engage your warm audiences, you're going to help convert those stragglers, right? That might need some extra convincing into actually purchasing your product or service, right? So you've got convert prospects into buyers, another goal, re-engage and continue to build the relationship, right? Really the two goals for warm traffic campaigns and then our hot audience, right? And again, we're living up here at the end of the customer journey. This is pretty simple. This is your buyer's list, right? If you have people that have already purchased your product or service, that is your hot audience. And I will tell you this is the most underutilized audience when it comes to traffic temperature. When most people are thinking about paid traffic, like I said earlier, they're going out to those cold audiences which is great. But don't forget that you can still use Facebook and Google and YouTube to re-target your hot traffic to get them to buy again or to get them to buy something else, right? You just have to remember that your hot traffic audience only scales as much as your warm audience, only scales as much as your cold audience. So we want to make sure that we have a paid traffic campaign at this step of the journey, but we also want to acknowledge that the other campaigns, just like the assembly line that we talked about, are the ones that are passing prospects and customers down to our hot audience, right? Traffic temperature, really simple concept, but it gives us a language to understand where the audiences that we're targeting on these paid traffic platforms, where they fall in

the value journey, right? And what the goals of those campaigns should be.
